preFix.title: Fitting angle bead for a plastered corner
preFix.subtitle: Set a metal or plastic bead to form a sharp external plaster corner
preFix.excerpt: How to cut and fix a metal or plastic angle bead to an external plasterwork corner, bed it in plaster dabs, check it is plumb, and use it as a screed guide for floating the plaster to a clean right-angle arris.
preFix.sourceNotes: British Gypsum White Book technical guidance. Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering syllabus.

content.text: converts an uncertain plaster corner into a reliable straight

. The critical operation is setting the bead plumb and at the correct projection from the wall so that it becomes the screed edge for the finishing coat on both faces. A bead set out of plumb or at the wrong depth is the most common cause of a twisted or underplastered corner.
Step 1, measure and cut the bead

Measure the height of the corner from floor to ceiling or to the top of the plasterwork. Cut the angle bead to length with a pair of tin snips or the utility knife, cutting across the perforated wings and scoring through the nose. A clean square cut ensures the bead sits tight at the top without a gap. Standard bead stock is 2.4 m; cut from the top of the length, keeping the factory-cut square end at the bottom.
Brush any loose plaster or dust from the corner and dampen the substrate. Mix a small amount of finish or bonding plaster to a stiff consistency. Apply three

to each face of the corner: top, middle, and bottom: slightly proud of the finished plaster plane. The dabs support the bead while you plumb it; they should be stiff enough to hold the bead without slumping but wet enough to allow small adjustments.

Press the angle bead nose-first onto the corner, bedding the wings into the dabs. Hold the spirit level against the nose of the bead and check plumb on both the face plane and the profile plane: a bead can be plumb on the face but twist left or right. Adjust by pressing or easing. Check that the bead nose sits at a consistent distance from the substrate across its full height; this distance equals the intended plaster coat thickness. Tamp the wings further into the dabs where needed. Allow to set for 15 to 20 minutes before plastering.
Step 4, fill the wings and float to the bead

Apply the plaster coat to both faces of the corner, working from the bead outward and using its nose as the screed depth guide. Flatten the plaster flush with the bead nose using the trowel held at a shallow angle with one edge running along the bead. The bead nose and the plaster face should be exactly flush: proud plaster will leave a lip that reads as a shadow under oblique light. Scratch the coat if a second finish coat is to follow.

PVC angle bead is the preferred material in areas with high humidity or where the wall may flex (timber-frame construction), as it does not rust and is slightly flexible. For renders on external walls, stainless steel or PVC render bead is used: standard galvanised steel rusts through an external render within a few years. Some plasterers bed beads in adhesive tile dab rather than plaster dab, which gives a longer setting time for adjustment.

attrs.fix: Stop and allow the dabs to set fully — 20 to 30 minutes. If the bead has moved out of plumb, re-tap it into position immediately before the dabs set.
attrs.cause: Plaster dabs too wet and not firm enough when plastering started; bead not pressed firmly into the dabs.

attrs.fix: Treat with a rust inhibitor, allow to dry, and seal with a shellac primer before repainting. Replace with PVC bead if the problem recurs.
attrs.cause: Galvanised steel bead corroding, especially in a damp room such as a bathroom.
attrs.symptom: Rust stain bleeding through paint or plaster near the corner

attrs.fix: Sand the corner with a sanding block once fully cured to bring the plaster flush with the bead nose. The metal nose itself cannot be sanded back, so the plaster each side must be feathered in.
attrs.cause: Plaster applied proud of the bead nose; or nose of bead not used as a screed guide.
attrs.symptom: Arris is not sharp — corner has a slightly rounded profile after plastering

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.9 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
